phrases.text_local: Ce parc est vraiment charmant. J'adore l'atmosphère tranquille.
phrases.pronunciation: suh PARK ay vray-MAHN shar-MAHN. zhah-DOR laht-moss-FAIR trahn-KEEL.
phrases.cultural_note: The French concept of 'la flânerie' — leisurely strolling without a destination — was elevated to an art form by nineteenth-century Parisian poets. Woodland Park is perfect for it.

phrases.text_local: Est-ce qu'il y a un sentier de promenade dans ce parc ?
phrases.pronunciation: ess-keel ee ah un sahn-TYAY duh prom-NAHD dahn suh PARK ?
phrases.cultural_note: Walking paths in French parks are called 'sentiers' or 'allées.' The word 'promenade' (walk/stroll) is of French origin and is used in English too — a lovely linguistic connection to share.

responses.french: Oui, il y a un sentier qui fait le tour du parc, environ deux kilomètres.
responses.english: Yes, there's a trail that goes all the way around the park, about two kilometers.
responses.phonetic: WEE, eel ee ah un sahn-TYAY kee fay luh TOOR doo PARK, ahn-vee-ROHN duh kee-loh-MET-ruh.

responses.french: Le sentier commence juste derrière le terrain de jeux.
responses.english: The trail starts just behind the playground.
responses.phonetic: luh sahn-TYAY koh-MAHNS zhoos duh-RYAIR luh tair-AN duh ZHU.

phrases.text_local: Quel beau temps aujourd'hui ! Il fait vraiment doux.
phrases.pronunciation: kel BOH TAHN oh-zhoor-DWEE ! eel fay vray-MAHN DOO.
phrases.cultural_note: Talking about the weather ('parler de la météo') is a universally safe conversation topic in France, just as in the US. 'Il fait doux' (it's mild) is a particularly lovely expression.

phrases.intent_en: Greet a neighbor walking their dog
phrases.text_local: Bonjour ! Votre chien est adorable. Comment s'appelle-t-il ?
phrases.pronunciation: bohn-ZHOOR ! votr SHYAN ay ah-doh-RAH-bluh. koh-MAHN sah-PEL-teel ?
phrases.cultural_note: The French are famously devoted to their dogs ('les chiens') — France has one of the highest rates of dog ownership in Europe. Complimenting someone's dog is an instant social lubricant.

responses.french: Merci ! Il s'appelle Milo. Il adore se promener ici.
responses.english: Thank you! His name is Milo. He loves walking here.
responses.phonetic: mair-SEE ! eel sah-PEL MEE-loh. eel ah-DOR suh pro-muh-NAY ee-SEE.

responses.french: Oh, vous êtes trop gentil ! Elle s'appelle Luna.
responses.english: Oh, you're too kind! Her name is Luna.
responses.phonetic: oh, vooz et troh zhahn-TEE ! el sah-PEL LOO-nah.

phrases.intent_en: Tell someone you're going for a picnic
phrases.text_local: On va faire un pique-nique dans le parc. Vous avez des recommandations pour un bon endroit ?
phrases.pronunciation: on vah fair un peek-NEEK dahn luh PARK. vooz ah-VAY day ruh-koh-mahn-dah-SYON poor un bohn ahn-DRWAH ?
phrases.cultural_note: 'Le pique-nique' is actually a French word that English borrowed! French culture elevates the picnic to an art form — a proper one includes cheese, wine, and a proper tablecloth. Even in Kentucky.

responses.french: Il y a un coin d'herbe à l'ombre près du lac — c'est parfait pour un pique-nique.
responses.english: There's a shady grassy spot near the pond — it's perfect for a picnic.
responses.phonetic: eel ee ah un KWAN dairb ah LOHM-bruh pray doo LAK — say par-FAY poor un peek-NEEK.
